section.title: The Time of the End
section.sectionRationale: The book closes on its highest note. Beyond the wars and the tyrants comes the greatest deliverance of all: Michael arises, God's people whose names are in the book are rescued, and the dead awake, some to everlasting life and some to everlasting contempt. This is the clearest promise of resurrection in the Old Testament. Daniel is told to seal the words, to rest, and to rise at last to his inheritance, ending the book with a personal pledge of resurrection hope.
section.thesis: After the final distress, Michael stands up and God's people are delivered, the dead awake to everlasting life or everlasting contempt, the wise shine like the stars, and Daniel, though he does not fully understand, is sent on his way with the promise that he will rest and then rise to his inheritance at the end of the days.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Michael arises and the dead awake to everlasting life; seal the book until the time of the end; the sealed words that only the wise will understand; and the final promise, rest now, and rise to your inheritance.

units.label: Michael arises; the dead awake
units.note: everlasting life and the shining wise

verses.text: “At that time Michael, the great prince who stands watch over your people, will rise up. There will be a time of distress, the likes of which will not have occurred from the beginning of nations until that time. But at that time your people, everyone whose name is found written in the {{sepher|book}}, will be delivered.
And many who sleep in the dust of the earth will {{qits|awake}}, some to {{chayye_olam|everlasting life}}, but others to shame and everlasting contempt.
Then the {{maskilim|wise}} will shine like the brightness of the heavens, and those who lead many to righteousness will shine like the stars forever and ever.

units.label: Seal the book until the time of the end

verses.text: But you, Daniel, shut up these words and seal the book until the time of the end. Many will roam to and fro, and knowledge will increase.”
Then I, Daniel, looked and saw two others standing there, one on this bank of the river and one on the opposite bank.
One of them said to the man dressed in linen, who was above the waters of the river, “How long until the fulfillment of these wonders?”

And the man dressed in linen, who was above the waters of the river, raised his right hand and his left hand toward heaven, and I heard him swear by Him who lives forever, saying, “It will be for a time, and times, and half a time. When the power of the holy people has finally been shattered, all these things will be completed.”
I heard, but I did not understand. So I asked, “My lord, what will be the outcome of these things?”
“Go on your way, Daniel,” he replied, “for the words are closed up and sealed until the time of the end.
Many will be purified, made spotless, and refined, but the wicked will continue to act wickedly. None of the wicked will understand, but the wise will understand.
And from the time the daily sacrifice is abolished and the abomination of desolation set up, there will be 1,290 days.
Blessed is he who waits and reaches the end of the 1,335 days.

units.label: Rest, and rise to your inheritance
units.note: you will arise at the end of the days

verses.text: But as for you, go on your way until the end. You will rest, and then you will arise to your inheritance at the end of the days.”

sepher.gloss: A book, scroll, written record. Deliverance belongs to everyone whose name is found written in the book, the heavenly register of God's people. The image runs through Scripture as the book of life; final salvation rests not on human strength but on being known and recorded by God.

qits.gloss: To awake, to rouse from sleep. Those who sleep in the dust of the earth will awake, a plain picture of bodily resurrection. This is the Old Testament's clearest promise that death is a sleep from which God will raise His people, some to everlasting life and others to judgment.

chayye_olam.gloss: Everlasting life, life of the age to come. This is the only place in the Hebrew Scriptures where the exact phrase appears; the resurrected righteous awake to chayye olam. Jesus takes up the very promise, offering everlasting life to all who believe in Him, John 5:28-29.

maskilim.gloss: The wise, those who have insight. The maskilim who suffered in chapter 11 now shine like the brightness of the heavens and like the stars forever. Their wisdom is faithfulness to God that leads many to righteousness, and it is crowned with resurrection glory.

basic.context: The final vision reaches its climax. Michael, the guardian of God's people, rises up at a time of unequaled distress, and all whose names are written in God's book are delivered. Then comes the great promise: the dead will awake, some to everlasting life and some to everlasting shame. The wise who taught righteousness will shine like stars forever. Daniel is told to seal the words until the end, is assured the wise will understand, and is sent to rest and finally to rise to his inheritance.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter gives the Old Testament's clearest teaching on bodily resurrection and final judgment, taken up directly by Jesus. The mysterious numbers and the time, times, and half a time are held with humility; the certainties are the resurrection, the two destinies, and the vindication of the faithful. The book ends not with a decoded timetable but with a personal promise of resurrection to Daniel himself.
basic.originalLanguages: Deliverance is for those written in the sepher, the book of life. The dead will qits, awake, from the dust, some to chayye olam, everlasting life, the only place this exact Hebrew phrase appears. And the maskilim, the wise, will shine like the stars forever.
basic.typology: The resurrection promised here is secured and inaugurated by Christ, the firstfruits of those who sleep. The wise who lead many to righteousness and shine like stars foreshadow all who, in Christ, turn many to righteousness and will share His glory. Daniel resting and rising points to the believer's death and resurrection in Jesus.
basic.christ: Jesus quotes this chapter, promising that all in the tombs will hear His voice and come out, some to the resurrection of life. He is the one who brings the everlasting life Daniel foresaw, having risen first Himself. The book of life is the Lamb's book, and the deliverance of God's people is accomplished by Christ, who conquered death.
basic.god: God is the living One who reigns beyond death, keeps a book of His people, and will raise the dead and judge the world. He sets the times, permits a final distress, and delivers all who are His. He refines His people through suffering and crowns the faithful with resurrection glory, so that death is not the end but a sleep before waking.
basic.crossReferences: John 5 has Jesus promising resurrection to life or judgment, echoing this chapter. First Corinthians 15 proclaims Christ the firstfruits of resurrection. And Revelation 20 shows the book of life opened at the last judgment.
basic.oneStory: The whole book has moved through empires and tyrants toward this: a final deliverance that death itself cannot stop. God's people awake to everlasting life, the wise shine like stars, and Daniel is promised his own resurrection. This hope is secured in Christ, the risen firstfruits, and it carries every believer through distress to the day they too will rise to their inheritance.

context.literary: The conclusion of the final vision and of the whole book. It moves from the last distress and Michael's rising, to the resurrection and the shining of the wise, to the sealing of the words, and finally to a tender personal word to Daniel. It ties together the book's threads: the wise of chapter 11, the guardian Michael of chapter 10, and the appointed times throughout.
context.historical: Written to sustain God's people through persecution, the chapter looks beyond any single crisis to the ultimate resurrection and judgment. The numbers of days extend the earlier figures tied to the desecration of the temple, likely offering the suffering faithful a bounded period to endure while pointing past it to the end.
context.canonical: Daniel 12 is the Old Testament's fullest statement of personal, bodily resurrection to everlasting life or contempt, and Jesus draws on it directly. The shining of the wise like stars is echoed in the Gospels, and the book of life and final judgment run on into Revelation, making this chapter a bridge to the New Testament's resurrection hope.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lift the suffering people of God beyond every earthly deliverance to the final one, the resurrection of the dead and the vindication of the faithful, and to seal the vision with a personal promise to Daniel that he will rest and rise, so that God's people endure in resurrection hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's certain future acts, resurrection and judgment; it is not a puzzle for calculating dates from its numbers. Its enduring calls are to be among the wise who lead many to righteousness, to be purified through trial, and to wait in hope for the resurrection.

debates.question: Is the awakening in verse 2 a bodily resurrection or a figure for national revival?

views.label: A literal, bodily resurrection to two destinies
views.case: The language of sleeping in the dust and awaking to everlasting life or everlasting contempt is personal and final, and Jesus quotes it in John 5:28-29 of the resurrection of life and of judgment; this is the natural reading and the mainstream Christian one.

views.label: Primarily a figure of national restoration
views.case: A minority, noting apocalyptic imagery elsewhere for the revival of a people (as in Ezekiel 37's dry bones), read the awakening first as the restoration of the nation; yet even they usually grant it points beyond itself to personal resurrection.

debates.bearing: The overwhelming weight, confirmed by Jesus's own use, is that this is a real resurrection of individuals to everlasting life or judgment, the hope that anchors the whole book and every believer.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's hope is carried by four words. Deliverance belongs to all whose names are written in the sepher, the book of God's people, so that salvation rests on being known and recorded by God. The dead will qits, awake, from the dust of the earth, a plain image of bodily resurrection, sleep broken by God's summons. They awake, the righteous, to chayye olam, everlasting life, the only appearance of that exact phrase in the Hebrew Scriptures, a phrase Jesus fills to the full. And the maskilim, the wise who suffered and led many to righteousness, now shine like the brightness of the heavens and like the stars forever. The book that opened in the darkness of exile ends in a blaze of resurrection light.

typology.type: The dead awaking from the dust to everlasting life
typology.fulfillment: The resurrection Christ secures and inaugurates as the firstfruits, and will complete at His coming, 1 Corinthians 15:20-23.

typology.type: The wise who shine like the stars forever
typology.fulfillment: The righteous who, in Christ, will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father, Matthew 13:43.

typology.type: Daniel resting and rising to his inheritance
typology.fulfillment: The believer's death as sleep and resurrection to the inheritance kept in Christ, 1 Thessalonians 4:14-16 and 1 Peter 1:4.

study.christ: Jesus takes the promise of this chapter onto His own lips: an hour is coming when all who are in the tombs will hear the voice of the Son of Man and come out, those who did good to the resurrection of life and those who did evil to the resurrection of judgment. He is the one who gives the everlasting life Daniel foresaw, for He Himself rose first as the firstfruits of those who sleep, conquering death. The book in which the names of the delivered are written is the Lamb's book of life, and the great distress is answered by the greater deliverance He accomplishes. Daniel's closing promise, you will rest, and then you will arise, is the gospel hope: to die in Christ is to sleep, and to sleep in Him is to be certain of rising to an inheritance that never fades.
study.god: God is the living One, who lives forever and reigns beyond the grave, who keeps a book of His people's names and will raise the dead to everlasting life or contempt. He appoints the times and permits a final distress, yet He delivers all who are His and sends His great prince Michael to stand for them. He refines the faithful through suffering and crowns them with the shining glory of the stars. Because He is the God of resurrection, death for His people is only a sleep, and the end of the days is not loss but inheritance.

crossReferences.note: Jesus promises all in the tombs will hear His voice and rise to life or judgment, quoting Daniel 12:2.
Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection, guaranteeing the awakening Daniel foresaw.
The righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father, echoing the shining wise.
The books and the book of life opened at the final judgment, the register of the delivered.

study.oneStory: The whole book has journeyed through falling empires and defiant tyrants toward this final scene: a deliverance that even death cannot undo. God's people, written in His book, awake from the dust to everlasting life; the wise shine like stars forever; and Daniel is given a personal pledge that he too will rest and rise to his inheritance. This resurrection hope is secured in Christ, the risen firstfruits, who carries every believer through every distress to the day they will wake to everlasting life at the end of the days.

quiz.q: What happens to God's people at the time of great distress?

options.text: Michael arises to stand for them, and everyone whose name is written in the book is delivered.
options.note: Daniel 12:1 promises deliverance for those written in the book.

options.text: They are all wiped out by the tyrant.
options.note: The opposite; those in the book are delivered.

options.text: They are left with no help at all.
options.note: Michael, their great prince, rises up to defend them.

options.text: They must save themselves by force.
options.note: Deliverance comes from God through Michael, not by their own force.

quiz.q: What does verse 2 teach about the dead?

options.text: Many who sleep in the dust will awake, some to everlasting life and some to everlasting contempt.
options.note: Daniel 12:2 is the Old Testament's clearest resurrection promise.

options.text: The dead simply cease to exist forever.
options.note: The verse teaches resurrection to two destinies, not annihilation for all.

options.text: Only the wicked are raised, to be judged.
options.note: Both the righteous and the wicked awake, to life or to contempt.

options.text: No one will ever rise from death.
options.note: The verse plainly promises the dead will awake.

quiz.q: What is promised to the wise who lead many to righteousness?

options.text: They will shine like the brightness of the heavens and like the stars forever and ever.
options.note: Daniel 12:3 crowns the faithful wise with shining glory.

options.text: They will be forgotten after they die.
options.note: The opposite; they shine forever, remembered and glorified.

options.text: They will rule earthly kingdoms in this life.
options.note: Their reward is everlasting shining glory, not earthly rule.

options.text: They will receive nothing special.
options.note: They are promised to shine like the stars forever.

quiz.q: How did the vision end for Daniel personally?

options.text: He was told to go his way, rest, and then rise to his inheritance at the end of the days.
options.note: Daniel 12:13 gives him a personal promise of resurrection.

options.text: He was told he would fully understand every detail at once.
options.note: He was told the words were sealed; his comfort was the promise of rising, not full understanding.

options.text: He was condemned for his many questions.
options.note: He was gently sent on his way with a promise, not condemned.

options.text: He was told there is no hope beyond death.
options.note: He was promised he would rest and rise to his inheritance.

quiz.q: How does this chapter's resurrection hope point to Christ?

options.text: Jesus quotes it, promising all in the tombs will hear His voice and rise, and He rose first as the firstfruits.
options.note: John 5:28-29 and 1 Corinthians 15 tie this hope to Christ's resurrection.

options.text: It teaches resurrection is only a symbol with no reality.
options.note: Jesus treats it as real bodily resurrection, secured in His own rising.

options.text: It predicts only Daniel would ever rise.
options.note: It promises resurrection for many, fulfilled and shared through Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: Jesus directly draws on this chapter for His resurrection promise.

questions: Daniel promises the dead will awake to everlasting life. How does the certainty of resurrection change the way you face death, your own or a loved one's?
Deliverance belongs to those whose names are written in the book. What comfort do you find in resting your salvation on God's record rather than your own strength?
The wise who lead many to righteousness will shine like stars. Whom is God calling you to point toward Him, whatever it costs you now?
Daniel heard but did not fully understand, and was told to go his way in trust. Where do you need to be at peace with not understanding all of God's plan?
The book ends with rest now and rising later. How does the promise that you will rise to your inheritance steady you through present distress?

translationNotes.note: Everlasting life renders a phrase found only here in the Hebrew Scriptures; Jesus takes it up in John 5:29 for the resurrection of life and of judgment.
The wise shining like the brightness of the heavens is echoed by Jesus in Matthew 13:43, the righteous shining like the sun in the Father's kingdom.
A time, times, and half a time is the same symbolic span as in chapter 7, a limited period of distress fixed by God, sworn by the one who lives forever.
The 1,290 and 1,335 days extend the earlier figures tied to the temple's desecration; the exact reckoning is held with humility, and the blessing rests on those who faithfully wait.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Daniel 12:1-13. Em dashes in verse 1 converted to commas. Chapter 12 is Hebrew, so load-bearing lexicon entries use Hebrew. No em or en dashes used.
